coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of the corrosion inhibition behavior of N, S co-doped memory-type carbon dots for mild steel in HCl solution
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Lei
Zhu, Mengyue
Shi, Wei
Wang, Kai
Leng, Senlin
Brahmia, Ameni
coredata.dc:description: Novel N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with memory effect were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid and l-cysteine as precursors. Structural and morphological characterizations confirmed that N, S-CDs are carbon dots with multifunctional groups and excellent water solubility. Electrochemical measurements indicated that N, S-CDs can effectively inhibit the charge transfer in the corrosion process of steel, and the inhibition efficiency is nearly 90%. The obtained N, S-CDs exhibits outstanding inhibition performance for steel corrosion, which offers a new thought for exploring environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Electrochemistry
Mild steel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and morphological characterization of N, S-CDs (a) FTIR spectra; (b) UV–vis spectra; (c) XRD pattern; (d) TEM image.
(a) Polarization, (b) Nyquist, (c) Bode plots, and (d) equivalent circuit diagrams for the absence and presence of N, S-CDs, respectively.

SEM and AFM images of the mild steel surface (a, d) Polished, (b, e) blank solution, and (c, f) with 150 mg/L N, S-CDs; XPS wide scan spectra (f), deconvoluted profiles of (h) N 1 s, and (i) S 2p for mild steel with N, S-CDs.
Electrochemical parameters for mild steel in 1 M HCl with and without N, S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of the corrosion inhibition behavior of N, S co-doped memory-type carbon dots for mild steel in HCl solution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with memory effect were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid and l-cysteine as precursors. Structural and morphological characterizations confirmed that N, S-CDs are carbon dots with multifunctional groups and excellent water solubility. Electrochemical measurements indicated that N, S-CDs can effectively inhibit the charge transfer in the corrosion process of steel, and the inhibition efficiency is nearly 90%. The obtained N, S-CDs exhibits outstanding inhibition performance for steel corrosion, which offers a new thought for exploring environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel is one of the most important metal materials due to its good mechanical properties and low price. However, it is susceptible to corrosion in acidic environments. Among the strategies for preventing steel corrosion, corrosion inhibitors are regarded as one of the most effective anti-corrosion methods due to their low dosage and simple operation [1]. The acidic cleaning inhibitors usually contain heteroatoms (O, S, N) and conjugated-bonds. Adding them to the corrosion media can form a barrier film on the metal surface through physical/chemical interactions, block the active sites and slow down the corrosion rate. In recent years, zero dimensional carbon quantum dots have been widely investigated in the application of corrosion inhibitors due to their low toxicity, good water solubility, and chemical stability [2]. The surface of carbon dots is easy to modify, and abundant active groups can be grafted. Especially, these functional groups have memory effect and can exhibit similar activities to the precursors. For instance, Wu et al. reported the synthesis of N-CDs using citric acid and tetraethylenepentamine as raw materials by microwave method. The results indicated that N-CDs can effectively improve the corrosion resistance of N80 steel in saturated CO2 saline solution, and the inhibition efficiency is 83.5 % when the dosage of N-CDs is 600 mg/L [3]. Pan and co-workers prepared N, S-CDs using thiourea and 4-aminosalicylic acid as precursors, and its inhibition efficiency for Mg alloy in NaCl solutioncan can reach 86.6 % at 50 mg/L [4]. Ye et al. obtained N-CDs through the pyrolysis of ammonium citrate at 180 °C, and the corrosion inhibition effect on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution exceeded 90 % at 200 mg/L [5]. However, the influence of the structures of carbon dots on the inhibition performance is still unclear, and further experimental research is still necessary for the development of carbon dots-based corrosion inhibitors.

Herein, we prepared N, S co-doped memory-type carbon dots with multiple active centers using citric acid and l-cysteine. The corrosion inhibition performance and adsorption mechanism of N, S-CDs on mild steel in 1 M HCl solution were explored by electrochemical measurements and surface analyses.

4.8450 g (0.04 mol) of l-cysteine and 4.2043 g (0.02 mol) of citric acid were respectively weighed and dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water, and then the prepared solution was sonicated for 2 min to completely dissolve the solutes. The mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon reactor, heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The reacted solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 D for 24 h to remove small molecules that did not participate in the reaction in the reactant. The dialyzed aqueous solution was freeze-dried for 72 h, and a brown product that N, S-CDs was finally obtained.

The structures of N, S-CDs were characterized by FTIR (IRTracer-100) and UV–vis (Tu-1901) measurements. The crystal structure and morphological size distribution were verified by XRD (6100) and TEM (JEM-2010). Electrochemical measurements were performed in 1 M HCl. Platinum and calomel electrodes were chosen as auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests were performed between 0.25 V to −0.25 V relative to open circuit potential. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were recorded in the 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz range with a 10 mV AC perturbation.

The FTIR spectra of N, S-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. The broad peaks at high wavenumbers of 3435 cm−1 and 3152 cm−1 correspond to the − OH and − N H stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 2926 cm−1, 1705 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, 1576 cm−1, 1396 cm−1, and 1076 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of C H, CO, N − CO, CN, C N, and CS bond, respectively [6]. The bending vibration of the aromatic C H bond appears as an absorption peak at 783 cm−1. From the UV–vis spectra in Fig. 1b, two significant peaks at 230 nm and 343 nm are detected, belonging to π-π* electronic transition of conjugated CC bonds and n-π* electronic transition of CN /CO bonds. Graphene like structure was seen from the XRD diffraction pattern (Fig. 1c), and an broad amorphous peak (at 2θ = 22°) was observed, indicating that the crystallinity decreased and amorphous CDs are formed. From the TEM micrograph in Fig. 1d, it is evident that the CDs are unevenly spherical in shape and range in size from 3.1 to 7.8 nm.

The polarization results for mild steel in 1 M HCl in the absence and presence of N, S-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 a and Table 1 . Overall, the corrosion potential (E corr) shifts towards the positive direction, and the displacement in E corr between uninhibited and inhibited conditions is less than 85 mV, suggesting that N, S-CDs in this work act as a mixed-type inhibitor dominated by anodic type, which is consistent with the Tafel slopes (β a, βc) changes [7]. After adding N, S-CDs, the polarization resistance (R p) gradually increases, while the corresponding corrosion current densities (i corr) and corrosion rate (C R) values were greatly decreased. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η PDP) is as high as 89.6 % at low concentration, which proves that N, S-CDs has an effective inhibitory effect on steel corrosion in acidic medium.

Compared with the blank solution, the Nyquist curves (Fig. 2b) after adding N, S-CDs not only has a capacitive reactance loop in the high frequency (HF) region but also has an inductive loop in the low frequency (LF) region. Bode plots (Fig. 2c) clearly observed that with the increase of N, S-CDs concentration, the phase of angles and impedance modulus values also increased. It can be seen from the EIS parameters in Table 1, which were fitted by the corresponding equivalent circuit in Fig. 2d, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) increases significantly with the introduction of N, S-CDs. The increase in R ct suggests the increase in the thickness of the adsorption film, which implies that N, S-CDs replace the pre-adsorbed water molecules to form a thicker adsorption film on the metal surface, thereby reducing the double-layer capacitance (C dl) and enhancing the inhibition of steel corrosion on the metal surface. More detailed theorems and formulas about electrochemical analysis were given in the supporting information.

The surface morphology of the polished mild steel (Fig. 3 a) are only scratches caused by polishing on the surface. Severe corrosion pits were obviously observed on the surface of mild steel immersed in blank solution (Fig. 3b). On the contrary, after the addition of N, S-CDs, the corrosion pits on the surface of mild steel were significantly reduced. As can be seen from the inset of Fig. 3c, the contact angle of the steel surface after adding N, S-CDs is larger than that of the blank solution, which indicates that the adsorption film of N, S-CDs on the surface of carbon steel increases the surface hydrophobicity. Fig. 3(d-f) shows the 3D corrosion morphologies of the carbon steel surface. The corresponding roughness values are 27.01 nm, 199.1 nm, and 104.2 nm, respectively. The large reduction in the roughness of the corroded surface can directly indicate that N, S-CDs effectively slow down the corrosion of carbon steel surface. In the comparison of XPS spectra (Fig. 3g), it can be observed that the immersed surface containing N, S-CDs has obvious peaks of N and S elements. The high resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of N 1 s and S 2p on the surface of mild steel after immersion in N, S-CDs are shown in Fig. 3h and Fig. 3i. The N 1 s spectra has two split peaks of 400.28 eV and 402.38 eV. These peaks are referred to N − Fe and CN+, respectively. The S 2p spectra are curve-fitted with three peaks. The peak at 163.1 eV, 165.43 eV and 168.85 eV can be associated with C S, Fe–S, and C − SO x (x = 2, 3, 4), respectively [8]. From XPS analysis, the N and S elements in N, S-CDs chemically react with Fe atoms on the surface of carbon steel to form an adsorption film that inhibits corrosion.

Electrochemical measurements indicated that the prepared memory-type N, S-CDs have a good corrosion inhibition effect on mild steel in HCl solution. This zero-dimensional nanomaterial presents excellent corrosion resistance, which makes them potentially promising in anti-corrosion applications of metals.

Lei Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Mengyue Zhu: Methodology, Investigation. Wei Shi: Project administration. Kai Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Senlin Leng: Resources, Supervision. Ameni Brahmia: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ameni Brahmia extends appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through the research group under grant number (RGP. 2/175/43). This work is supported by the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou Province (QJHKY[2020]067, QJJ[2022]003), the Science and Technology Program of Guizhou Province (QKHPTRC[2021]5643, QKHZK[2022]555), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M710117), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22262030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.133403.
